perturbation.original_text: XFN may terminate this Agreement by giving thirty (30) days written notice to the CEIS.
perturbation.changed_text: XFN may terminate this Agreement by giving ten (10) days written notice to the CEIS.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the notice period for termination from 30 days to 10 days may conflict with standard commercial contract laws that require reasonable notice, especially considering the long-term nature of the agreement and the potential damages that CEIS could face if XFN cancels the contract with little notice. What constitutes 'reasonable notice' depends on the jurisdiction, but 10 days is an unusually short period for a long-term contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law - Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification shortens the termination notice to 10 days, which could be deemed unreasonable and contradict the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing in contract law, especially since this contract is for 20 years. While no single law mandates a specific notice period, contract law principles require a reasonable timeframe, which 10 days may not fulfill, particularly considering CEIS's potential losses.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 30 days to 10 days, which could be deemed unreasonable. UCC § 2-309(3) states that 'Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party'. The modified contract could be viewed as providing an unreasonably short notification period, particularly for a potentially long-term agreement, thus creating a legal contradiction. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight the potential unreasonableness of the 10-day notice.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of Hong Kong
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is governed by and shall be construed in accordance with the laws of North Korea.
perturbation.explanation: Selecting North Korean law as the governing law would likely be unenforceable in many jurisdictions, especially if either party has significant ties to countries with established legal systems. Moreover, North Korea's legal system is not transparent or predictable, making contract enforcement extremely difficult and potentially violating public policy concerns in other countries.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles and Public Policy
perturbation.law_explanation: The altered text designates North Korean law as governing, potentially violating public policy principles. Courts may refuse to enforce a choice-of-law provision if it contravenes fundamental principles of justice in the forum state. North Korea's legal system is opaque and lacks the rule of law, so it is against what countries such as the US and Hong Kong view as just.

perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces Hong Kong law with North Korean law, creating a significant issue. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that North Korea's legal system is opaque and lacks the rule of law, which contradicts principles of justice. Although the scraped snippets are not helpful, the explanations provided indicate that designating North Korean law as governing would be against what countries such as the US and Hong Kong view as just, thus making it unenforceable and creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each notice, demand or other communication given or made under this Agreement shall -6- be in writing and delivered or sent to the relevant Party's Managing Director or General Manager at its aforesaid address (or such other address as the addressee may specify by five days' prior written notice to the other Party).
perturbation.changed_text: Each notice, demand or other communication given or made under this Agreement shall -6- be in writing and delivered or sent to the relevant Party's Managing Director or General Manager at its aforesaid address (or such other address as the addressee may specify by one day's prior written notice to the other Party).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for address changes from five days to one day could be deemed unreasonable, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. If a critical notice is sent to an outdated address and not received due to the extremely short notice period, it could lead to legal complications. It is unreasonable that if a notice is given one day before, the other party has to change their records to accomodate for this change of address.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of Contract Law - Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text reduces the required notice for address changes to one day, which may be deemed unreasonable and conflict with the duty of good faith. UCC 1-309 implies a reasonable time for notification, but a one-day notice is likely insufficient for organizations to update records and ensure important communications are received, therefore breaching the implied duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-309. Option to Accelerate at Will. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-309. Option to Accelerate at Will. A term providing that one party or that party's successor in interest may accelerate payment or performance or require collateral or additional collateral "at will" or when the party "deems itself insecure," or words of similar import, means that the party has power to do so only if that party in good faith believes that the prospect of payment or performance is impaired. The burden of establishing lack of good faith is on the party against which the power has been exercised. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notice period for address changes from five days to one day. The Law Explanation states that UCC 1-309 implies a reasonable time for notification, and a one-day notice is likely insufficient, breaching the implied duty of good faith. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC 1-309, discusses good faith in the context of accelerating payment. While not directly about notice periods, it underscores the legal importance of good faith. The one-day notice contradicts the implied duty of good faith, as explained in the Law Explanation, making the change a contradiction. The inconsistency creates a potential for legal complications, especially if critical notices are sent to the outdated address and not received due to the short notice period. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition where a time-sensitive obligation does not align with legal requirements.
